metadata.title: Control of Nitrosamine Impurities in Human Drugs

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers and applicants of APIs and drug products must conduct risk assessments to detect and prevent unacceptable levels of nitrosamine impurities in drug products; the recommendations apply to all chemically synthesized APIs, drug products containing chemically synthesized APIs or fragments, and semisynthetic and fermentation products at risk due to their structures.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: This guidance recommends steps manufacturers and applicants of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and drug products should take to detect and prevent unacceptable levels of nitrosamine impurities in drug products.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers and applicants must implement the three-step mitigation strategy: (1) conduct risk assessments of approved or marketed products and products with pending applications, (2) perform confirmatory testing if a risk is identified, and (3) report changes to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities to FDA in accordance with applicable requirements.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction; Section IV.B — Recommended Three-Step Mitigation Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: this guidance discusses potential root causes of nitrosamine formation and advises API and drug product manufacturers and applicants that they should use the three-step mitigation strategy described in this guidance to (1) conduct risk assessments of their approved or marketed products and products with pending applications, (2) perform confirmatory testing if a risk is identified, and (3) report changes to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities to FDA in accordance with applicable requirements.

rules.rule_text: An acceptable intake (AI) limit for nitrosamines is a level that approximates an increased cancer risk of one additional case in 100,000 subjects based on a conservative assumption of daily exposure to a mutagenic impurity over a lifetime (70 years).
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits
rules.verbatim_quote: An AI limit as defined in ICH M7(R2) is a level that approximates an increased cancer risk of one additional case in 100,000 subjects based on a conservative assumption of daily exposure to a mutagenic impurity in drug substances and drug products over a lifetime (70 years).

rules.rule_text: FDA recommends that an AI limit be determined using the predicted Carcinogenic Potency Categorization Approach when robust carcinogenicity data and additional safety information on specific nitrosamine compounds are not available; the NDMA AI limit is 96 ng/day.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits; Appendix B
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that an AI limit be determined by using the predicted Carcinogenic Potency Categorization Approach when robust carcinogenicity data and additional safety information, including bacterial mutagenicity data, on specific nitrosamine compounds are not available (see the RAIL guidance for an explanation of the Carcinogenic Potency Categorization Approach).
rules.exceptions: If the AI limit cannot be determined using the approaches described, FDA recommends 26.5 ng/day be used as the AI limit.
cross_references: RAIL guidance (Recommended Acceptable Intake Limits for Nitrosamine Drug Substance-Related Impurities August 2023)

rules.rule_text: If the AI limit cannot be determined using described approaches, FDA recommends that 26.5 ng/day be used as the AI limit.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits
rules.verbatim_quote: If the AI limit cannot be determined using the approaches described in this section, FDA recommends that 26.5 ng/day be used as the AI limit.

rules.rule_text: When more than one nitrosamine is identified in a drug product, the total limit of nitrosamines should not exceed the recommended AI limit for the most potent nitrosamine in the drug product.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that when more than one nitrosamine is identified, the total limit of nitrosamines should not exceed the recommended AI limit for the most potent nitrosamine in the drug product.
rules.exceptions: If the recommended AI limits for individual nitrosamines vary greatly, it might not be practical to base the total nitrosamine limit on the most potent individual limit and an alternative approach (flexible AI limit approach per Appendix C) might be appropriate.

rules.rule_text: Recommended AI limits are typically determined in units of nanograms per day (ng/day); conversion of an AI limit into parts per million (ppm) for specification limits is calculated as: ppm = AI (ng/day)/MDD (mg), where MDD is the maximum daily dose reflected in the drug product labeling.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits
rules.verbatim_quote: Recommended AI limits reflect a permissible level of exposure to the impurity and are typically determined in units of nanograms per day (ng/day). For the purpose of establishing controls such as specification limits, conversion of an AI limit into parts per million will vary by product and is calculated based on a drug's MDD (parts per million = AI (ng/day)/MDD (mg)).

rules.rule_text: An alternative flexible AI limit approach can be used to establish specifications when multiple nitrosamine impurities are present in drug products, where each individual nitrosamine is calculated as a percentage of its AI limit and the sum must not exceed 100 percent.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits; Appendix C
rules.verbatim_quote: An alternative flexible AI limit approach can be used to establish specifications when multiple nitrosamine impurities (e.g., both small-molecule nitrosamines and NDSRIs) are present in drug products. Using the approach described in Appendix C, a manufacturer or applicant can ensure that the levels of the nitrosamine impurities when totaled result in an exposure level that does not exceed the acceptable cancer risk of 1:100,000 as outlined in ICH M7(R2).

rules.rule_text: Sensitive analytical methods with appropriate limits of quantitation (LOQs) are needed to test whether drug products can meet the AI limits; the detection and quantitation limit should be commensurate with the level at which impurities must be controlled, in accordance with ICH Q2(R1).
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Acceptable Intake Limits
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, sensitive analytical methods with appropriate limits of quantitation (LOQs) are needed to test whether drug products can meet the AI limits recommended for nitrosamine impurities. In accordance with the ICH guidance for industry Q2(R1) Validation of Analytical Procedures: Text and Methodology (November 2005), the detection and quantitation limit should be commensurate with the level at which the impurities must be controlled. The LOQ should be scientifically justified based on these principles.
cross_references: ICH Q2(R1) Validation of Analytical Procedures

rules.rule_text: Risk assessments for nitrosamine impurities should be retained as documents at the facility (not required to be submitted to FDA proactively) and must be available if requested.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Recommended Three-Step Mitigation Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk assessments should be conducted in a timely manner based on the prioritization of drugs. Manufacturers and applicants do not need to submit risk assessment documents to the Agency, but they should retain these documents so that they are available if requested.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory testing for nitrosamines must have specificity, excellent chromatographic separation, and highly sensitive detection capability due to nitrosamines' physiochemical properties (low molecular weights, some volatility, and high toxicity).
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Recommended Three-Step Mitigation Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to nitrosamines' physiochemical properties (low molecular weights, some volatility, and high toxicity), the analytical test methods for nitrosamines need to have specificity, excellent chromatographic separation, and highly sensitive detection capability.

rules.rule_text: Changes implemented to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities in APIs and drug products must be reported to FDA, including submission of DMF amendments under 21 CFR 314.420(c), and changes to approved applications as required under 21 CFR 314.70, 21 CFR 314.97, 21 CFR 601.12, and pending applications under 21 CFR 314.60 and 21 CFR 314.96.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Recommended Three-Step Mitigation Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: Report changes implemented to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities in APIs and drug products to FDA. This includes submission of any drug master file (DMF) amendments in accordance with 21 CFR 314.420(c) and changes to approved applications as required under 21 CFR 314.70, 21 CFR 314.97, and 21 CFR 601.12 and pending applications under 21 CFR 314.60 and 21 CFR 314.96.

rules.rule_text: API manufacturers must review their API manufacturing processes and conduct risk assessments to identify the potential for nitrosamine impurities; all manufacturers of chemically synthesized APIs should take appropriate actions to mitigate the risk where there is potential for nitrosamine formation.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Recommendations for API Manufacturers
rules.verbatim_quote: all manufacturers of chemically synthesized APIs should take appropriate actions to mitigate the risk of nitrosamine impurities in APIs where there is a potential for nitrosamine formation. API manufacturers should review their API manufacturing processes and conduct risk assessments to identify the potential for nitrosamine impurities.

rules.rule_text: If a nitrosamine impurity is detected in an API, the API manufacturer should investigate the root cause, implement changes to reduce or prevent the nitrosamine from forming, and notify drug product manufacturers that have been supplied the affected API lots.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Recommendations for API Manufacturers
rules.verbatim_quote: If a nitrosamine impurity is detected, API manufacturers should investigate the root cause. They should implement appropriate follow-up actions, including changes in the manufacturing process to reduce or prevent nitrosamine impurities from forming (see section V.). The Agency encourages API manufacturers to notify through the field alert reporting system if initial assessments and testing show nitrosamine formation in APIs that have been sold for use in the U.S. market and to inform drug product manufacturers that have been supplied the API lots.
cross_references: FDA guidance Field Alert Report Submission: Questions and Answers (July 2021)

rules.rule_text: API manufacturers should optimize the design of the manufacturing process for APIs during route of synthesis (ROS) development to minimize or prevent formation of nitrosamine impurities, including avoiding reaction conditions that produce nitrosamines, using non-amine bases when ROS conditions may form nitrosamines, and replacing nitrites with other quenching agents for azide decomposition.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1 — Mitigating the Presence of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: API manufacturers should optimize the design of the manufacturing process for APIs during route of synthesis (ROS) development to minimize or prevent the formation of nitrosamine impurities... Avoiding reaction conditions that may produce nitrosamines whenever possible; when not possible, demonstrating that the process is adequately controlled... Using bases other than secondary, tertiary, or quaternary amines (when possible) if ROS conditions may form nitrosamines... Replacing nitrites with other quenching agents for azide decomposition processes.
cross_references: ICH Q7 Good Manufacturing Practice Guidance for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients
ICH Q11 Development and Manufacture of Drug Substances

rules.rule_text: API manufacturers should audit their supply chains and monitor them for at-risk API raw materials and intermediates, maintaining records of supplier names and any repackers or distributors; additional specifications for at-risk materials should be established to prevent formation of nitrosamine impurities.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1 — Mitigating the Presence of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: API manufacturers should audit their supply chains and monitor them for any at-risk API raw materials and intermediates. API manufacturers should maintain records including the name(s) of the raw material or intermediate supplier(s) and the raw material or intermediate manufacturer(s) (if not the supplier), and any repackers and distributors who handle the materials before API manufacture. When appropriate, API manufacturers should establish controls and consider additional specifications for at-risk materials to prevent formation of nitrosamine impurities.

rules.rule_text: To avoid cross-contamination, API manufacturers should use recovered materials (solvents, reagents, catalysts) only in the same step or an earlier step of the same process from which the material was collected; recovered materials must meet appropriate standards before reuse.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1 — Mitigating the Presence of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: To avoid cross-contamination when recovered materials such as solvents, reagents, and catalysts are used in the manufacturing process, API manufacturers should use recovered material only in the same step or in an earlier step (if there is sufficient purification) of the same process from which it was collected. The recovered materials should meet appropriate standards before reuse.

rules.rule_text: API manufacturers should analyze nitrite and nitrosamine levels in water used for manufacturing and use water purified to remove unacceptable impurities, as potable water may contain low levels of nitrites and nitrosamines from environmental sources.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.1 — Mitigating the Presence of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: API manufacturers should be aware that potable water used in API manufacture may contain low levels of nitrites and even nitrosamines from environmental sources. The existence of nitrites in processing water may lead to nitrosamine formation in API manufacture. Therefore, to avoid unacceptable levels of nitrosamine impurities in APIs, API manufacturers should analyze nitrite and nitrosamine levels in water and use water that has been purified to remove unacceptable impurities.

rules.rule_text: If a nitrosamine impurity is detected in an API above the LOQ, the API manufacturer must develop a control strategy to ensure the nitrosamine level reliably remains at or below the recommended AI limit, taking into account batch-to-batch variations.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.2 — Control of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: If a nitrosamine impurity is detected above the LOQ, the API manufacturer should develop a strategy to ensure that the nitrosamine level remains at or below the recommended AI limit. Manufacturers should develop an appropriate control strategy to ensure that the nitrosamine level reliably remains below the recommended AI limit in the API, taking into account batch-to-batch variations.

rules.rule_text: For at-risk APIs with an impurity detected above 10 percent of the recommended AI limit, manufacturers must test each batch on release and the stability samples on the retest dates for the presence of nitrosamine impurities.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.2 — Control of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: Given existing uncertainties regarding nitrosamine impurities and their presence in drugs, for at-risk APIs with an impurity detected above 10 percent of the recommended AI limit, manufacturers should test each batch on release and the stability samples on the retest dates for the presence of nitrosamine impurities.

rules.rule_text: Any API batch found to contain levels of nitrosamine impurities above the recommended AI limit must not be released by the API manufacturer for distribution.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C.2 — Control of Nitrosamine Impurities in APIs
rules.verbatim_quote: Any API batch found to contain levels of nitrosamine impurities above the recommended AI limit should not be released by the API manufacturer for distribution.
rules.exceptions: The API manufacturer should contact the Agency if the API may be needed to prevent a disruption in the drug supply.

rules.rule_text: Drug product manufacturers and applicants must test representative samples of all incoming components, including lots of at-risk API, before use, as required under 21 CFR 211.84; they must continue testing each API lot for nitrosamine impurities until they have verified that the API supplier can consistently manufacture API meeting the recommended AI limits.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.2.a — Establishing Reliability of API Suppliers
rules.verbatim_quote: Drug product manufacturers and applicants must test representative samples of all incoming components, including lots of at-risk API, before use, as required under 21 CFR 211.84. To meet the current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) regulations in 21 CFR part 211, subpart E, and be consistent with the ICH guidance for industry Q10 Pharmaceutical Quality System (April 2009), drug product manufacturers and applicants should continue testing each API lot for nitrosamine impurities until they have verified that the API supplier can consistently manufacture API that meets the recommended AI limits throughout its expiry or retest date.

rules.rule_text: The control strategy for drug products must include specification limits for identified nitrosamine impurities if confirmatory testing finds levels above 10 percent of the recommended AI limit, including in stability samples at expiry.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.2.b — Establishing Specifications in Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: The control strategy should include specification limits for the identified nitrosamine impurities in the drug product if the confirmatory testing finds levels of the nitrosamine impurity above 10 percent of the recommended AI limit, including in stability samples at expiry.

rules.rule_text: Any drug product batch found to contain levels of a nitrosamine impurity above the FDA recommended AI limit must not be released by the drug product manufacturer for distribution and may warrant removal from the market as such products may be considered adulterated under section 501 of the FD&C Act.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.2.b — Establishing Specifications in Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, any drug product batch found to contain levels of a nitrosamine impurity above the FDA recommended AI limit should not be released by the drug product manufacturer for distribution and may warrant removal from the market, because such drug products may be considered adulterated under section 501 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), for example if they are not manufactured, processed, packed, or held in conformity with CGMP.
rules.exceptions: FDA may exercise enforcement discretion when warranted to prevent or mitigate a shortage of a drug.

rules.rule_text: NDA and ANDA holders are required to submit field alert reports as described in 21 CFR 314.81(b)(1) if testing shows that any batch of distributed drug product fails to meet specifications on nitrosamine impurities; BLA holders must report biological product deviations as described in 21 CFR 600.14.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.2.b — Establishing Specifications in Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: applicants holding new drug applications (NDAs) and abbreviated new drug applications (ANDAs) are required to submit field alert reports as described in 21 CFR 314.81(b)(1) if testing shows that any batch of distributed drug product fails to meet the specifications established in the relevant application, including specifications on nitrosamine impurities. Similarly, applicants that hold a biologics license application are required to report biological product deviations as described in 21 CFR 600.14.

rules.rule_text: To prevent or reduce the presence of NDSRIs in drug products, manufacturers should screen excipients using a supplier qualification program accounting for nitrite impurities across excipient suppliers and lots, and may alternatively reformulate with alternate excipients that have lower nitrite levels.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.3 — Recommendations for Drug Product Manufacturers on Reduction or Prevention of NDSRI Formation
rules.verbatim_quote: Screen excipients using a supplier qualification program that accounts for potential nitrite impurities across excipient suppliers and excipient lots to reduce the risk of NDSRI formation in drug products. Alternatively, reformulate the drug product with an alternate excipient that has lower levels of nitrites.

rules.rule_text: Drug product manufacturers should consider designing drug products incorporating an antioxidant (such as ascorbic acid, ascorbate salt, alpha-tocopherol, or propyl gallate) which may inhibit NDSRI formation in drug products.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.3 — Recommendations for Drug Product Manufacturers on Reduction or Prevention of NDSRI Formation
rules.verbatim_quote: Design drug products incorporating an antioxidant (such as ascorbic acid (vitamin C), ascorbate salt (such as sodium ascorbate), alpha-tocopherol, or propyl gallate), which may inhibit NDSRI formation in drug products. Recent research has demonstrated that the addition of a small amount of antioxidants to formulations may significantly inhibit the formation of NDSRIs in drug products (Shakleya et al. 2023).

rules.rule_text: For approved or marketed drug products with small molecule nitrosamine impurities, manufacturers and applicants must have concluded risk assessments by March 31, 2021, and must conclude confirmatory testing and submission of required changes in drug applications by October 1, 2023.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.1 — Approved or Marketed Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: For small molecule nitrosamine impurities, as previously communicated, FDA recommended that manufacturers and applicants conclude a risk assessment of approved or marketed products by March 31, 2021... FDA recommended that manufacturers and applicants conclude confirmatory testing of drug products and submission of required changes in drug applications for small molecule nitrosamines by October 1, 2023.

rules.rule_text: For NDSRIs not previously considered in risk assessments, manufacturers and applicants must complete a risk assessment by November 1, 2023; NDSRI confirmatory testing and submission of required changes in drug applications must be concluded by August 1, 2025.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.1 — Approved or Marketed Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommended that if NDSRIs were not considered in previous risk assessments, manufacturers and applicants complete a risk assessment for NDSRIs by November 1, 2023, as part of overall risk management. FDA recommends conclusion of NDSRI confirmatory testing of drug products and submission of required changes in drug applications by August 1, 2025.

rules.rule_text: Prospective applicants must conduct a scientifically based risk assessment for nitrosamine impurities in APIs and proposed drug products and perform confirmatory testing before submission of an original NDA or BLA application; if not available at submission, risk assessment and confirmatory testing can be submitted in an amendment as quickly as possible.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.2.a — Presubmission Stage
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that applicants conduct a scientifically based risk assessment for nitrosamine impurities in APIs and proposed drug products and perform confirmatory testing as appropriate before submission of an original application. However, the risk assessment and submission of confirmatory testing, if appropriate, and changes to the DMF or application can be submitted in an amendment if they are not available at the time of the original submission filing. Such an amendment should be submitted as quickly as possible after the original submission filing.

rules.rule_text: If confirmatory testing indicates nitrosamine levels exceed 10 percent but are within the recommended AI limit, a control for nitrosamines must be established in release and stability specifications; for approved products this must be submitted in a supplement as changes being effected in 30 days.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Assessment of Test Results
rules.verbatim_quote: If the confirmatory testing indicates nitrosamine levels exceed 10 percent of the recommended AI limit but are within the recommended AI limit, a control for nitrosamines should be established in the release and stability specifications. For approved drug products, this information should be submitted in a supplement as changes being effected in 30 days.

rules.rule_text: If confirmatory testing indicates nitrosamine levels exceed the recommended AI limit and changes in formulation, manufacturing process, or packaging are warranted, these changes must be submitted to the Agency in a prior approval supplement when they meet the criteria described in 21 CFR 314.70(b) or 601.12(b) as major changes.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Assessment of Test Results
rules.verbatim_quote: If the confirmatory testing indicates nitrosamine levels exceed the recommended AI limit and changes in formulation, manufacturing process, or packaging are warranted, manufacturers and applicants should implement such changes that are demonstrated to ensure that nitrosamine levels remain within the recommended AI limit. For approved drug products, when these changes meet the criteria described in § 314.70(b) or § 601.12(b) as major changes, they must be submitted to the Agency in a prior approval supplement.

rules.rule_text: If a manufacturer proposes an alternative AI limit or identifies an NDSRI not on the nitrosamine guidance web page, a proposed AI limit or predicted carcinogenic potency category associated with an AI limit must be submitted to FDA for evaluation; for approved products this must be submitted in accordance with 21 CFR 314.70(b) or 601.12(b).
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Assessment of Test Results
rules.verbatim_quote: If a manufacturer or applicant proposes an alternative AI limit for nitrosamine impurities or identifies an NDSRI that is not included on the nitrosamine guidance web page, a proposed AI limit or predicted carcinogenic potency category associated with an AI limit should be submitted to FDA for evaluation. For approved drug products, this information must be submitted in accordance with § 314.70(b) or § 601.12(b).

rules.rule_text: Applicants must report changes implemented to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities in accordance with FDA regulations; API DMF holders making process changes must submit a DMF amendment per 21 CFR 314.420(c) and inform each drug product manufacturer or applicant that references the DMF.
rules.source_section: Section V.C — Reporting Changes to Mitigate Nitrosamine Impurities
rules.verbatim_quote: Applicants must report changes implemented to prevent or reduce nitrosamine impurities in accordance with FDA regulations (§§ 314.60, 314.70, 314.96, 314.97, and 601.12). Holders of pending applications must update their applications through submission of an amendment according to §§ 314.60 and 314.96. If an API DMF holder makes process changes in the ROS as a result of the risk assessment and confirmatory testing, the DMF holder must submit an amendment to the DMF and inform each drug product manufacturer or applicant that references the DMF (including those with pending and approved applications), in accordance with § 314.420(c).

rules.rule_text: Reformulation of an approved drug product by adding antioxidants or pH modifiers to control nitrosamines is generally considered a major change requiring a prior approval supplement under 21 CFR 314.70(b)(1) and 314.97(a).
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2 — Reporting Changes for Drug Products Reformulated to Mitigate Nitrosamines
rules.verbatim_quote: As provided in §§ 314.70(b)(1) and 314.97(a), changes to an approved NDA or ANDA that have a substantial potential to have an adverse effect on the identity, strength, quality, purity, or potency of the drug product (i.e., a 'major change') require submission and approval of a supplement before distribution. Reformulation of an approved drug product is generally considered a major change requiring a prior approval supplement.

rules.rule_text: For reformulated drug products addressing NDSRIs, stability data from three batches are required; for approved products at supplement submission, stability data for 3 months at accelerated conditions (time points 0, 1, 2, and 3 months) and long-term (0 and 3 months) stability conditions must be provided.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.a — Stability Data to Support Reformulated Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that such changes be supported by manufacture and testing of samples from three batches of drug products. For approved drug products, at the time of supplement submission, applicants should provide stability data for 3 months at accelerated (time points 0, 1, 2, and 3 months) and long-term (0 and 3 months) stability conditions for these three batches.
rules.exceptions: For application products, FDA may also request 6 months of accelerated stability data before approval if analysis shows potential for an upward trend in NDSRI levels.
cross_references: ICH Q1A(R2) Stability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products

rules.rule_text: For IR solid oral or IR oral suspension drug products with BCS I, II, or III APIs reformulated with specific antioxidants (ascorbic acid, alpha-tocopherol, propyl gallate, or cysteine hydrochloride) at no more than 10 mg per dose or maximum daily exposure, comparative dissolution testing may be sufficient to demonstrate BA or BE without an in vivo study.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.b — Bioequivalence Studies for Reformulated Products
rules.verbatim_quote: If the product contains an API that is classified as BCS I, II, or III, and the reformulation incorporates a pH modifier, or one of the specific antioxidants studied in FDA-supported research (ascorbic acid, α-tocopherol, propyl gallate, or cysteine hydrochloride), in an amount no more than 10 mg per dose or maximum daily exposure (whichever is lower), submitting comparative dissolution testing data on the pre- and post-change products (in quality control dissolution medium and multimedia of pH 1.2, 4.5 and 6.8) may be sufficient to demonstrate BA or BE.
rules.exceptions: Does not apply to narrow therapeutic index drug products or BCS IV APIs; modified-release products require in vivo BE studies.
cross_references: ICH M9 Biopharmaceutics Classification System-Based Biowaivers

rules.rule_text: Drug products that contain or fail to adequately address the risk of nitrosamine impurities may not meet applicable requirements of the FD&C Act, including sections 501 and 505; such products would violate the Act if not manufactured, processed, packed, or held in conformity with CGMP.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Assessment of Test Results
rules.verbatim_quote: Drug products that contain or fail to adequately address the risk of nitrosamine impurities may not meet applicable requirements of the FD&C Act, including sections 501 and 505. For example, such products would violate the Act if they are not manufactured, processed, packed, or held in conformity with CGMP in accordance with section 501(a)(2)(B).

rules.rule_text: Relevant records demonstrating compliance with CGMP requirements for nitrosamine control must be made available for FDA review during an inspection or when requested by FDA; manufacturers must be prepared to provide test results and root cause analyses.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Assessment of Test Results
rules.verbatim_quote: Relevant records and other information that demonstrate compliance with CGMP requirements must be made available for FDA review during an inspection conducted under section 704(a)(1) of the FD&C Act or when requested by FDA in advance or in lieu of an inspection as described in section 704(a)(4) of the FD&C Act. Manufacturers should contact FDA at CDER-OPQ-inquiries@fda.hhs.gov when testing reveals that nitrosamine impurities in a drug product exceed FDA's recommended AI limits. Manufacturers should also be prepared to provide FDA with test results and root cause analyses in such cases.

rules.rule_text: NDSRIs are a class of nitrosamines sharing structural similarity to the API having the API or an API fragment in the chemical structure and are generally unique to each API; drug products containing APIs with secondary, tertiary, or quaternary amine groups are considered at risk for NDSRI formation if exposed to a nitrosating agent.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.2 — NDSRIs; Section III.D — Root Causes of the Presence of NDSRIs in Drug Products
rules.verbatim_quote: NDSRIs are a class of nitrosamines sharing structural similarity to the API (having the API or an API fragment in the chemical structure) and are generally unique to each API. NDSRIs form through nitrosation of APIs (or API fragments) that have secondary, tertiary, or quaternary amines when exposed to nitrosating compounds such as nitrite impurities in excipients... Drug products containing APIs with secondary, tertiary, or quaternary amine groups are considered at risk for NDSRI formation if exposed to a nitrosating agent.
rules.exceptions: APIs with tertiary amine functional groups inherently have lower risk for nitrosamine formation than those with secondary amines; quaternary amines have even lower risk.
cross_references: RAIL guidance (Recommended Acceptable Intake Limits for Nitrosamine Drug Substance-Related Impurities, August 2023)
